ml.heading: Neural Performance & Analysis
ml.subtitle: Monitor the clinical performance and neural attention of your specialized AI models.
ml.accuracy_desc: % of total predictions that were correct
ml.precision_desc: % of positive predictions that were actually positive
ml.f1score_desc: Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall
ml.auc_desc: Area Under the ROC Curve — model's ability to distinguish classes
ml.roc_subtitle: Diagnostic ability of the binary classifier system
ml.heatmap_subtitle: Upload an image to see exactly which clusters triggered the AI diagnostic sequence.
ml.gradcam_desc: GradCAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) highlights the regions of your image that the AI model focused on most when making its prediction.
ml.drop_medical: Drop medical-grade image or browse disk
ml.awaiting_desc: Upload medical forensic scan to initialize high-fidelity neural mapping.

diet: Eat iron-rich foods: spinach, lentils, beans, tofu
Include Vitamin C with every meal — lemon juice, oranges, guava
Add beetroot and pomegranate to your daily diet
Consume dates, raisins, and figs as snacks
Include fortified cereals and whole grains
Eat dark leafy greens: methi, palak, drumstick leaves
supplements: Iron supplements only if prescribed by doctor
Vitamin B12 — found in eggs, milk, paneer
Folate-rich foods: broccoli, avocado, peas
Vitamin C supplement 500mg can enhance iron absorption
Multivitamin with iron — consult pharmacist
lifestyle: Get 7-8 hours of sleep every night
Light walking 20-30 minutes daily
Avoid strenuous exercise until levels improve
Stay hydrated — drink 2-3 liters of water daily
Manage stress with yoga or meditation
precautions: Avoid tea and coffee immediately after meals — blocks iron absorption
Do not self-medicate with iron supplements without blood test
Get CBC blood test to confirm anemia diagnosis
Monitor symptoms: fatigue, dizziness, pale skin
Cook in iron cookware to increase dietary iron

diet: Significantly increase iron intake — red meat, chicken liver, oysters
Eat iron-rich vegetarian foods 3x daily: rajma, chana, soya
Pair every iron-rich meal with Vitamin C source
Drink beetroot-carrot juice daily
Include moringa (drumstick) leaves in meals
Avoid calcium-rich foods alongside iron-rich foods
supplements: Ferrous sulfate 325mg — only with doctor's prescription
Vitamin B12 injection if deficiency confirmed
Folic acid 400-800 mcg daily as advised
Iron + Vitamin C combination tablets
Get CBC test before starting any supplement
lifestyle: Rest adequately — avoid overexertion
Gentle yoga: Pranayama, Shavasana for energy
Short walks only — avoid high intensity exercise
Elevate feet when resting to improve circulation
Track energy levels and fatigue daily
precautions: Consult a hematologist within 2 weeks
Do not delay CBC blood test — confirm diagnosis
Watch for symptoms: severe fatigue, breathlessness, rapid heartbeat
Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs — can worsen bleeding
Avoid alcohol completely during recovery
Iron supplements can cause constipation — increase fiber

diet: Start iron-rich medical diet immediately under dietitian guidance
Organ meats daily if non-vegetarian: liver, kidney
Fortified foods: iron-fortified milk, cereals, bread
Small frequent meals — 5-6 times a day
Smoothies with spinach, beetroot, dates, banana
Avoid foods that block iron: tea, coffee, dairy with meals
supplements: ⚠️ URGENT: See doctor immediately for iron infusion evaluation
Prescription iron therapy — oral or IV as prescribed
B12 injections if pernicious anemia suspected
Erythropoietin therapy if recommended by specialist
Do not self-treat — this level requires medical supervision
lifestyle: Strict rest — minimize physical activity
No strenuous exercise until hemoglobin improves
Breathing exercises only — Anulom Vilom, deep breathing
Sleep 9-10 hours per day
Use wheelchair or support if severe dizziness occurs
precautions: 🚨 Seek immediate medical attention — high risk anemia
Blood transfusion may be needed — consult hematologist
Do not drive or operate machinery
Monitor hemoglobin weekly with blood tests
Emergency symptoms: fainting, severe breathlessness — go to ER
Avoid all blood-thinning medications without doctor approval

diet: Follow low glycemic index (GI) diet
Eat whole grains: brown rice, oats, barley, millet
Include fenugreek seeds (methi) daily — reduces blood sugar
Bitter gourd (karela) juice in the morning
Avoid white rice, maida, sugary drinks, sweets
Eat small meals every 3-4 hours to maintain stable glucose
supplements: Chromium — helps insulin sensitivity
Magnesium — found in nuts, seeds, dark chocolate
Berberine supplement — consult doctor before use
Vitamin D3 — deficiency worsens insulin resistance
Cinnamon capsules — may help lower fasting glucose
lifestyle: Walk 30 minutes after each meal — most effective habit
Strength training 3x per week improves insulin sensitivity
Monitor fasting blood sugar every morning
Reduce screen time and sedentary behavior
Practice stress management — cortisol raises blood sugar
precautions: Get HbA1c test to confirm prediabetes or diabetes
Schedule annual eye exam for early retinopathy
Check feet daily for sores or numbness
Maintain healthy weight — even 5% reduction helps significantly
Avoid smoking — dramatically increases diabetes complications

diet: Strict diabetic diet — consult registered dietitian
Carbohydrate counting — limit to 45-60g per meal
No sugar, no refined carbs, no fruit juices
High fiber diet: psyllium husk, vegetables, legumes
Jamun seeds powder — traditional blood sugar remedy
Eat at fixed times every day — avoid skipping meals
supplements: Metformin or other medications — only as prescribed
Alpha-lipoic acid for nerve protection
CoQ10 — supports heart health in diabetics
Omega-3 fatty acids — fish oil or flaxseed
B-complex vitamins — prevent diabetic neuropathy
lifestyle: Daily 45-minute brisk walk — non-negotiable
Self-monitoring blood glucose (SMBG) 2-4 times daily
Keep a food and glucose diary
Quit smoking and alcohol immediately
Annual kidney function test (creatinine, eGFR)
precautions: Consult endocrinologist within 1 week
Get HbA1c test every 3 months
Check blood pressure regularly — target <130/80
Foot care: wash, dry, moisturize daily — inspect for wounds
Carry glucose tablets for hypoglycemia episodes
Never skip meals if on insulin or sulfonylureas

diet: Medical nutrition therapy — only follow dietitian's plan
Very low carbohydrate diet under medical supervision
Strictly avoid all forms of sugar and simple carbs
High protein, moderate fat, very low carb meals
Frequent small meals — never fast for long periods
Adequate hydration — 3+ liters water daily
supplements: 🚨 Insulin therapy likely needed — see endocrinologist immediately
Prescribed diabetes medications — strict adherence
Neuropathy supplements: B1, B6, B12 as prescribed
ACE inhibitors for kidney protection if prescribed
Do not stop or change medications without doctor approval
lifestyle: Medical supervision required for all exercise
Daily blood glucose monitoring — multiple times
HbA1c target: below 7% — work with doctor
Annual dilated eye exam — retinopathy risk is high
Podiatrist visit every 3-6 months for foot health
precautions: 🚨 Immediate medical consultation required
Risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — know the symptoms
Emergency signs: extreme thirst, confusion, vomiting — go to ER
Never skip insulin doses if prescribed
Wear diabetic footwear at all times
Carry medical ID identifying you as diabetic

diet: Protein-rich diet to promote wound healing: eggs, fish, legumes
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, beef, chickpeas
Vitamin C for collagen synthesis: citrus fruits, bell peppers
Vitamin A for skin healing: sweet potato, carrots
Stay well hydrated — aids tissue repair
Anti-inflammatory foods: turmeric, ginger, omega-3
supplements: Zinc 25-40mg daily — promotes wound healing
Vitamin C 500-1000mg daily
Vitamin E — topical and oral for skin repair
Collagen peptides supplement — aids tissue healing
Omega-3 fatty acids — reduces inflammation
lifestyle: Inspect feet daily with a mirror — check between toes
Keep feet clean and dry — wash with mild soap, pat dry
Moisturize feet daily — avoid applying between toes
Wear proper fitting diabetic socks and shoes
Elevate feet when sitting to improve circulation
precautions: Never walk barefoot — even indoors
Do not pop blisters or cut corns yourself
Trim toenails straight across — never curved
See podiatrist immediately if wound appears
Control blood sugar — high glucose slows healing

diet: High protein diet: 1.2-1.5g per kg body weight daily
Wound healing smoothie: protein powder + berries + spinach
Avoid sugar completely — impairs immune response
Bone broth — collagen and minerals for tissue repair
Iron-rich foods — prevents anemia which slows healing
supplements: ⚠️ Medical wound dressings only — no home remedies on wound
Prescribed antibiotics if infection present — complete full course
Zinc + Vitamin C + Vitamin E combination
L-Arginine supplement — improves blood flow to wound
Consult wound care specialist for dressing protocol
lifestyle: Offloading: use crutches, wheelchair, or special boot to keep weight off wound
Daily wound inspection — photograph and track healing progress
Keep wound covered with clean dressing at all times
Quit smoking immediately — dramatically impairs wound healing
Manage blood glucose aggressively — target HbA1c below 7%
precautions: See podiatrist or wound care specialist within 3 days
Watch for infection signs: increasing redness, warmth, pus, fever
Do not use antiseptics like Dettol directly on wound
Avoid tight shoes, socks, or anything that restricts blood flow
Hospital admission may be needed if infection spreads
Never delay treatment — DFU can worsen rapidly

diet: Hospital-level nutrition support likely needed
High calorie, high protein diet: 35 kcal/kg, 1.5g protein/kg
Enteral nutrition if oral intake is insufficient
Strict blood sugar control through diet and medication
No alcohol — severely impairs wound healing and immunity
supplements: 🚨 Immediate hospitalization may be required
IV antibiotics for deep infection
Surgical debridement may be needed
Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) consideration
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) for chronic ulcers
lifestyle: Bed rest likely required — strict pressure relief
Professional wound care multiple times weekly
Daily monitoring of vitals (temp, heart rate, BP)
Strict insulin management — IV insulin may be needed
Complete immobilization of affected limb if instructed
precautions: 🚨 CRITICAL: Risk of limb loss — surgical intervention needed
Sepsis awareness: fever, chills, rapid heartbeat — Emergency
Osteomyelitis (bone infection) screening required
Frequent vascular assessment (Doppler/ABI)
Do not touch wound or change surgical dressing without sterile prep
Immediate ER visit if wound foul-smelling or black (necrosis)

analysis_explanations.eye_anemia: The AI examined the color of your inner eyelid (conjunctiva). Healthy conjunctiva is pink-red due to good blood flow. Pale or white conjunctiva suggests low hemoglobin — a key sign of anemia. Our model identified pallor patterns in this region.
analysis_explanations.palm_anemia: The AI analyzed the skin color and texture of your palm. A healthy palm has pink coloration from blood flow through capillaries. Pale or yellowish palms indicate reduced red blood cells — a sign of anemia. The model detected reduced erythema in your palm.
analysis_explanations.retina_diabetes: The AI examined your retinal (fundus) image for signs of diabetic retinopathy. It looked for microaneurysms (tiny bulges in blood vessels), hemorrhages, and abnormal new vessel growth — all early signs of diabetes affecting the eyes.
analysis_explanations.skin_dfu: The AI analyzed your skin/foot wound image for Diabetic Foot Ulcer markers. It identified wound margin characteristics, tissue coloration patterns, and signs of poor healing typically associated with diabetic foot complications.
analysis_explanations.summary_desc: Scan type: {{type}} image. Primary clinical focus: {{diagnosis}}. AI risk assessment outcome: {{risk}}.

action_plan.title: Your Action Plan
action_plan.subtitle: Follow these steps based on your results

save_report.title: Save this report
save_report.desc: Download your PDF report and save it for your doctor's appointment.

see_doctor.title: See a doctor immediately
see_doctor.desc: Your results show high risk indicators. Please visit a hospital or clinic today.

cbc_test.title: Get a CBC blood test
cbc_test.desc: A Complete Blood Count test will confirm anemia and measure your hemoglobin level. Available at any diagnostic lab.

hba1c_test.title: Get an HbA1c blood test
hba1c_test.desc: This test measures your average blood sugar over 3 months. Available at any lab without fasting.

visit_podiatrist.title: Visit a podiatrist
visit_podiatrist.desc: A foot specialist (podiatrist) can assess the wound, provide dressing, and create a treatment plan.

follow_up.title: Schedule a follow-up scan
follow_up.desc: After treatment or lifestyle changes, run another MediScan analysis to track your progress.

results.consult_doctor_warning: Always consult a doctor before making health decisions
